home.title: Home
home.subtitle: Welcome to LikeAnOpenSource
intro: LikeAnOpenSource is a discovery and learning platform for people who want to explore open source with more clarity, more context, and better entry points.
Instead of dropping visitors into endless repository lists, the platform is designed to connect projects, articles, and guides into paths that actually help people move forward.

callout.text: Start with Explore for curated entry points, then move into Projects, Articles, and Guides depending on how deep you want to go.

groups.title: Platform Overview
intro: The experience is shaped around discovery, deep context, and practical next steps.

items.title: Curated entry points
items.description: Find strong starting points for open-source ecosystems, documentation stacks, developer tooling, and contributor-friendly communities.

items.title: Project collections with context
items.description: Browse repositories by category, maturity, onboarding quality, and the kind of learning value they offer.

items.title: Step-by-step paths
items.description: Follow guided routes built for first-time learners, active builders, and future contributors.

items.title: Deep technical context
items.description: Read long-form breakdowns that explain why projects matter, which tradeoffs they carry, and how teams actually use them.

items.label: For newcomers
items.text: Begin with Guides to build a mental model, then use Explore to find ecosystems and projects worth your time.

items.label: For active builders
items.text: Jump into Projects and Articles when you want to compare tools, evaluate architecture, or sharpen your workflow.

items.label: For contributors
items.text: Use the repository to suggest additions, improve content quality, and help shape better open-source onboarding.

items: Clarity matters more than hype. Every page should help visitors understand what something is, why it matters, and where to go next.
Good curation is more useful than volume. The goal is not to list everything, but to surface the most teachable, trustworthy, and well-explained things first.

blocks.text: The strongest open-source platforms do not just publish information. They reduce hesitation and make the next step feel obvious.

explore.title: Explore
explore.subtitle: How discovery is being shaped in LAOS
intro: Explore is where LAOS will eventually surface curated projects, ecosystems, and deep-dive writing.
Until the repository contains enough real records, this section should explain the discovery lens honestly instead of pretending there is already a populated feed.

groups.title: What Explore Surfaces
intro: These are the four discovery lanes the platform is intentionally building first.

items.title: Repositories with clear onboarding
items.description: Projects earn space here when setup, documentation, and contribution flow are understandable to someone new.

items.title: Stacks worth studying together
items.description: Explore should connect tools that make more sense as a system, such as docs platforms, frontend workflows, and developer tooling.

items.title: Deep dives with a point of view
items.description: Long-form writing here should explain tradeoffs, maintenance reality, and why a choice matters, not just list features.

items.title: Contribution paths that lower hesitation
items.description: Guides should turn curiosity into motion by showing where to start, what to improve, and how to contribute without guesswork.

items.label: Clear onboarding
items.text: A visitor should understand what the project is, how to start, and how much effort it asks from them.

items.label: Visible maintenance
items.text: There should be some sign of active stewardship, usable issue hygiene, or healthy release practices.

items.label: Learning signal
items.text: The project, article, or guide should teach something transferable instead of only promoting itself.

items.label: Honest tradeoffs
items.text: If something is powerful but difficult, expensive, or niche, Explore should say so clearly.

items.label: Contribution friendliness
items.text: The best candidates make it easier for a newcomer to understand where help is needed.

blocks.text: Explore should never simulate momentum. If something is featured, the team should be able to explain exactly why it deserves attention.

items: Right now the content work is focused on three foundations: honest repository evaluation, stronger getting-started guides, and long-form writing that connects tools to real workflows.
As real project records and articles land in the repository, Explore can stop describing the lens and start surfacing those records directly.

items.label: Documentation systems
items.text: We are prioritizing tools and patterns that solve navigation, search, structure, and authoring well.

items.label: Contributor-friendly repositories
items.text: We want to spotlight projects that make their contribution process visible and realistic for first-time contributors.

items.label: Workflow writing
items.text: The article layer is being shaped around real tradeoffs, maintenance lessons, and system-level thinking.

blocks.text: This area should stay focused on real project signals, clear selection criteria, and content that can stand behind its claims.

projects.title: Projects
projects.subtitle: Real repositories with clear learning value
intro: Projects should help visitors discover repositories through usability, purpose, and learning value instead of popularity alone.

items: This opening set is intentionally small. Each repository earns its place by teaching something concrete about docs architecture, frontend tooling, code quality, or terminal-based workflow design.
They are not here because they are simply well known. They are here because they give LAOS a strong starting point for explaining why certain repositories are worth studying.

items.label: Web and Frontend
items.text: Design systems, UI libraries, and framework-oriented repositories.

items.label: Documentation and Knowledge
items.text: Docs platforms, content tooling, search systems, and writing infrastructure.

items.label: CLI and Terminal
items.text: Tools that improve the command-line developer experience.

items.label: Devtools
items.text: Build, lint, test, and observability tooling.

items.label: Automation
items.text: Release helpers, workflow runners, generators, and repo maintenance utilities.

items.label: Community-first Repositories
items.text: Projects that stand out because of contribution quality, documentation clarity, or maintainer friendliness.

items: A strong project entry should help people decide whether something is worth learning from, contributing to, or adopting.
That means going beyond the repository name and showing why a project stands out in practice.

items.label: Why it matters
items.text: Explain the problem the project solves and who benefits from it.

items.label: Maintenance signal
items.text: Show whether the project has signs of active stewardship, healthy releases, and usable issue triage.

items.label: Learning value
items.text: Clarify whether the repository is interesting because of architecture, community process, or contributor experience.

items.label: Entry difficulty
items.text: Set expectations for setup complexity, docs quality, and how quickly a newcomer can become productive.

blocks.text: The best project catalogs do not just rank repositories. They reduce uncertainty and help people choose with confidence.

articles.title: Articles
articles.subtitle: Long-form writing with technical depth
intro: Articles are where the platform slows down and gives ideas enough room to breathe.

items: The article library should do more than explain how a tool works. It should surface tradeoffs, team impact, and the reasoning behind technical choices.
Strong editorial content helps people understand not only which project is interesting, but also which one fits their workflow, team, or stage of learning.

items.label: Title discipline
items.text: Every article should make its topic, scope, and intended reader clear from the first glance.

items.label: Context first
items.text: Good writing explains not only what to do, but also why a choice matters and what it may cost.

items.label: Cross-section visibility
items.text: Strong pieces should be easy to surface again inside Explore, Projects, or guide recommendations.

guides.title: Guides
guides.subtitle: Learning paths for real progress
intro: Guides should help people move from passive reading to confident action.

items.title: How to read the guides
items.description: Use the guide layer as a decision path. Start from your goal, stay inside one route, and branch into Projects or Articles only when you need depth.

items.title: How to contribute
items.description: Contribute where the reading experience feels weak: unclear copy, missing context, thin guide paths, or content that sounds more polished than it really is.

items.title: How to judge a repository quickly
items.description: Use the project criteria before you commit your time. The goal is to understand value, maintenance signal, and onboarding quality early.

items.label: Beginner
items.text: Fundamental concepts, terminology, and quick-start paths.

items.label: Intermediate
items.text: Architectural tradeoffs, process design, and project patterns.

items.label: Advanced
items.text: Maintainability, scale, and team knowledge transfer.

items: LikeAnOpenSource (LAOS) is a platform focused on explaining the ideas, design choices, and ecosystem patterns behind open source software.
It is not only a list of repositories. It is meant to connect projects with technical context, guide paths, and editorial storytelling.

items.label: Think like open source
items.text: Build knowledge in a modular, reusable, and transparent way.

items.label: Curate with care
items.text: A smaller, sharper set of recommendations is more valuable than an overwhelming wall of links.

items.label: Make contribution feel possible
items.text: People should leave the platform feeling closer to their first meaningful contribution, not further from it.

items: The platform should favor clear writing, visible reasoning, and recommendations that can be defended with real experience.

blocks.text: Quality here means trust. If something is featured, visitors should feel there is a good reason it earned that space.

items.title: Main repository
items.description: Follow the codebase, track how the platform evolves, and see where future content operations will live.

items.title: What ships next
items.description: The next phase is turning seeded local content into live repository-backed content so updates can flow from GitHub directly.
